Re
Fnrn PoLITTcAL PnacrrcEs CovrMrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
August 4,2003
Sandra Wallace
l2l Monterey Avenue
Capitola, CA 95010
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-03-111
Dear Ms. Wallace:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'Act').r
We have received your corespondence, including a letter from an appraiser
regarding the material financial effect of a decision to transfer elementary students from
Capitola Elementary School to other schools in the Soquel School District. You have
asked for us to reconsider your request taking into account this recent letter from the
appraiser. Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice
(In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), we cannot evaluate the factual accuracy of this
appraisal.2
As we advised in our previous letter, Wallace Advice Letter, No. 4-03-069,
obtaining an appraisal conducted by a disinterested and otherwise qualified real estate
professional will generally be considered a good faith effort by you to assess the financial
effect of the Capitola Elementary School decision on your residence, provided the
appraisal is based upon an accurate understanding ofthe underlying facts and considers
the factors such as those listed in the relevant materiality regulations 18705.2(b)(1XA) -
(C). (See Wallace, supra.) However, a public official is not required to obtain a
professional appraisal to assist in assessing the financial effect ofa decision for conflict
of interest purposes. (Condon Advice Letter, No. I-02-035; McHugh Advice Letter, No.
' Go.re.nment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
' While we do not evaluate the factual accuracy of the letter from Robert B. Curtis, dated May 15,
2003, which you submitted, we do point out that this letter does not base its conclusions on the factors
described in subdivisions (A ) - (C) of regulation 18705.2(bX1).
-@


File No. I-03-111
Page No. 2
A-02-021; Ho AdviceLetter,No. 4-00-241.) ltis up to the official to decide whether or
not to seek an appraisal.
As previously advised, in your situation, the presumption which must be
overcome is the presumption that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
any frnancial aflect on your property. "Any financial effect" includes an effect valued as
low as a penny's worth. As a result, you must determine that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that an effect valued at a penny's worth or more will result from the
elementary school decision-
Since this is a factual question (rather than a legal matter), it is incumbent upon
you to evaluate whether the appraisal analyzes available facts regarding the financial
effects, including but not limited to, the following:
"(A) The development potential or income producing
potential of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has
an economic interest;
(C) The characterof the neighborhood including butnot
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, pivacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood." (Regulation 187A5.2(bXt); see also
Wallace, supra.)
If you determine that the underlying facts on which the appraisal is based are
correct and that the opinion is a reasonable one based on a consideration of all relevant
factors, your reliance on the appraisal constitutes a good faith determination and you may
participate in the decision. (See Oderman Advice Letter, No. 4-00-082.) As we have
noted, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (Oglesby,
supra) and therefore cannot evaluate the factual accuracy of an appraisal. Ultimately,
you must make this determination.


File No. I-03-111
Page No. 3
If you have any other questions regarding this matteq please contact me at (916)
322-s660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
0c
Counsel, Legal
vlslon
Enclosure
NBje

sections.question_synthetic: Can a school board member participate in a decision to transfer elementary students when the member has a financial interest in real property that may be affected, and does obtaining a professional appraisal satisfy conflict-of-interest requirements under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A public official may rely on a disinterested professional appraisal as a good faith effort to assess financial effects of a decision, provided the appraisal is based on accurate facts and considers relevant factors including property development potential, use, and neighborhood characteristics. The official must ultimately determine whether the appraisal's underlying facts and conclusions are reasonable before participating in the decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter (File No. I-03-111) responding to a previous advice letter (Wallace No. 4-03-069). Document references an appraiser's letter submitted by requester but explains FPPC cannot evaluate factual accuracy. No verbatim QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; both synthesized from body text. Contains footnotes and regulatory references.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Can a school board member participate in a decision to transfer elementary students when the member has a financial interest in real property that may be affected, and does obtaining a professional appraisal satisfy conflict-of-interest requirements under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: A public official may rely on a disinterested professional appraisal as a good faith effort to assess financial effects of a decision, provided the appraisal is based on accurate facts and considers relevant factors including property development potential, use, and neighborhood characteristics. The official must ultimately determine whether the appraisal's underlying facts and conclusions are reasonable before participating in the decision.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Sandra Wallace's request regarding conflict-of-interest provisions when a school board member has potential financial interest in property affected by an elementary school transfer decision. The Commission clarifies that while obtaining a professional appraisal is not required, if obtained, it must be based on accurate facts and relevant materiality factors to constitute a good faith determination allowing participation.
